events.text: Patient is a 74-year-old White male of Hispanic ethnicity, height 5'7", weight 168 lbs, BMI 26.3, never smoker, history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and coronary artery disease, no family history of prostate cancer.

At age 65, patient was diagnosed with prostate adenocarcinoma (ICD-O site C61.9, histology 8140/3) on transurethral resection of the prostate performed for benign prostatic hyperplasia symptoms; PSA was 9.1 ng/mL.
At age 65, transurethral resection specimen showed incidental Gleason 3+4=7 (Grade Group 2) adenocarcinoma involving approximately 15% of the resected tissue; no perineural invasion.
At age 65, staging with bone scan and CT was negative for metastatic disease; patient was staged as cT1bN0M0.
At age 65, given the patient's age, comorbidities, and intermediate-risk disease, active surveillance was initially pursued with PSA monitoring every 3 months and repeat biopsy at 1 year.
At age 66, surveillance prostate biopsy showed upgrading to Gleason 4+4=8 (Grade Group 4) adenocarcinoma in 5 of 12 cores; treatment was recommended.
At age 66, patient received external beam radiation therapy (VMAT) to the prostate, total dose 78 Gy in 39 fractions, with concurrent ADT.
At age 66, leuprolide 22.5 mg intramuscular every 3 months was initiated concurrent with radiation, planned for 2 years given high-risk features.
At age 67, PSA nadir was 0.06 ng/mL on ADT; patient tolerated treatment well aside from grade 1 hot flashes.
At age 68, after completing 2 years of ADT, leuprolide was discontinued; PSA was 0.1 ng/mL.
At age 70, PSA rose to 3.2 ng/mL with testosterone recovery to 280 ng/dL; biochemical recurrence confirmed.
At age 70, leuprolide 22.5 mg intramuscular every 3 months was restarted for biochemical recurrence.
At age 71, PSMA PET/CT performed for rising PSA to 5.8 ng/mL on ADT showed PSMA-avid lesions in the T11 vertebral body and bilateral pelvic bones; no visceral disease.
At age 71, serum testosterone was 24 ng/dL with PSA of 5.8 ng/mL, confirming castration-resistant disease with new metastases.
At age 71, disease reclassified as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, stage rT1bN0M1b, with new bone metastases to thoracic spine and pelvis on continuous ADT.
At age 71, abiraterone acetate 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily was added to continued leuprolide for mCRPC.
At age 71, patient developed grade 2 hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 246 mg/dL) on abiraterone/prednisone requiring adjustment of diabetic medications; also grade 1 peripheral edema.

At age 71, next-generation sequencing (Guardant360) on circulating tumor DNA revealed PTEN homozygous loss, AR amplification, and TP53 Y220C mutation; no BRCA1/2 or ATM alterations; microsatellite stable.
At age 72, PSA declined to 1.8 ng/mL on abiraterone; bone pain was manageable with acetaminophen.
At age 73, restaging bone scan showed new uptake in the left 5th and 7th ribs and right proximal humerus in addition to known lesions; disease progression.
At age 73, PSA rose to 11.4 ng/mL on abiraterone; abiraterone was discontinued.
At age 73, docetaxel 75 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks with prednisone 5 mg twice daily was initiated.
At age 73, after cycle 1 of docetaxel, patient developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 peripheral neuropathy; also noted worsening glycemic control.
At age 74, palliative radiation therapy 8 Gy in a single fraction was delivered to the right proximal humerus for severe pain limiting arm function.
At age 74, patient sustained a compression fracture of T11 vertebral body; was started on denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks for skeletal-related event prevention.

scenario_blurb: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Rising PSA on ADT. Treated with enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, or cabazitaxel. Include BRCA1/2 and homologous recombination deficiency testing. Some with bone-predominant and some with visceral metastases.
